blocks.body: Countable vs Uncountable Nouns: Common Mistakes shows how not to mix up many / much, few / little, and how to handle nouns such as advice, information, homework correctly.

{"level":"A2-B1","intro":"The most common mistake here is not recognizing the noun type: can you count separate units, or are you talking about a mass, substance, or abstract idea?","rules":[{"label":"COUNTABLE","color":"emerald","text":"Countable nouns work with many, a few, number, singular/plural forms.","example":"many books / a few ideas / one student"},{"label":"UNCOUNTABLE","color":"blue","text":"Uncountable nouns often need much, a little, less, some and stay without a normal plural form.","example":"much time / a little money / some advice"},{"label":"PARTITIVES","color":"amber","text":"Words such as advice, information, homework, furniture often need a structure like a piece of or another measuring phrase.","example":"a piece of advice / three pieces of information"}]}

blocks.heading: 1. Where Learners Most Often Get Lost
blocks.body:
The problem usually starts when a word looks like an ordinary noun but behaves differently.

  • People, books, ideas, mistakes are countable, so they work naturally with many, few, numbers, and plural forms.
  • Advice, information, homework, furniture, luggage are uncountable, so they do not like forms such as an advice or informations.
  • Few / little without the article sound more negative than a few / a little.
  • Much appears more often in questions and negatives in everyday English, while positive statements often prefer a lot of.

blocks.heading: 2. How the Correct Choice Works
blocks.body:
Ask yourself three short questions.

  1. Can I count separate units? If yes, the noun is usually countable: one book, two books.
  2. Does the word describe a mass, substance, or abstract idea? If yes, it is often uncountable: money, time, music, advice.
  3. Do I need a partitive? If the noun is uncountable but you want one unit, add a phrase such as a piece of advice, a bit of information, a glass of water.

Compare: few students = not many students and this is a problem. a few students = several students and that may be enough. The same contrast works with little and a little.

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Turning an uncountable noun into a countable one with an article.","wrong":"I need an advice.","right":"I need some advice.","hint":"Advice is usually uncountable. If you need one unit, say a piece of advice."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Using much before a countable plural noun.","wrong":"There are not much people here.","right":"There are not many people here.","hint":"For countable nouns in the plural, use many, not much."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Adding a fake plural ending to an uncountable noun.","wrong":"She gave me many informations.","right":"She gave me a lot of information.","hint":"Information has no normal plural. Express quantity with some, a lot of, pieces of."},{"label":"Mistake 4","color":"rose","title":"Mixing up few and little.","wrong":"We have few milk left.","right":"We have little milk left.","hint":"Milk is uncountable, so it works with little / a little, not few / a few."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick takeaways","items":["Start by deciding whether the noun is countable or uncountable.","For countable plural nouns use many, few, a few; for uncountable nouns use much, little, a little.","Do not add a normal plural to words such as advice, information, homework, furniture.","When you need one unit of an uncountable noun, use a partitive: a piece of advice, a glass of water."]}
